section.title: Bless the Lord, O My Soul
section.sectionRationale: David's mature, sweeping hymn of pure gratitude moves from a soul stirred to remember God's kind deeds, forgiveness, healing, and compassion, out to God's covenant faithfulness across generations, and finally to the whole cosmos summoned to bless the LORD, its themes of steadfast love and complete forgiveness fulfilled in Christ.
section.thesis: Because the LORD forgives all iniquity, heals every disease, and shows compassion as high as the heavens and as far as the east is from the west, the whole soul, and indeed all creation, is summoned to bless His holy name and never forget His kind deeds.
section.frameNote: Four movements: a personal call to bless the LORD and remember His benefits, verses 1-5; God's righteous, compassionate character revealed to Moses and Israel, verses 6-14; human frailty set against the LORD's everlasting steadfast love, verses 15-19; and a cosmic chorus of angels, hosts, and all creation blessing the LORD, verses 20-22.

units.label: Do not forget all His kind deeds
units.note: a personal call to remember God's benefits

verses.text: Of David. {{barak|Bless}} the LORD, O my soul; all that is within me, bless His holy name.
Bless the LORD, O my soul, and do not forget all His kind deeds,
He who forgives all your iniquities and heals all your diseases,
who redeems your life from the Pit and crowns you with loving devotion and compassion,
who satisfies you with good things, so that your youth is renewed like the eagle's.

units.label: The Lord is compassionate and gracious
units.note: God's righteous character revealed to Moses and Israel

verses.text: The LORD executes righteousness and justice for all the oppressed.
He made known His ways to Moses, His deeds to the people of Israel.
The LORD is {{rachum|compassionate}} and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in {{chesed|loving devotion}}.
He will not always accuse us, nor harbor His anger forever.
He has not dealt with us according to our sins or repaid us according to our iniquities.
For as high as the heavens are above the earth, so great is His loving devotion for those who fear Him.
As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us.
As a father has compassion on his children, so the LORD has compassion on those who fear Him.
For He knows our frame; He is mindful that we are dust.

units.label: His loving devotion extends to those who fear Him
units.note: human frailty against God's everlasting love

verses.text: As for man, his days are like grass, he blooms like a flower of the field;
when the wind passes over, it vanishes, and its place remembers it no more.
But from everlasting to everlasting the loving devotion of the LORD extends to those who fear Him, and His righteousness to their children's children,
to those who keep His covenant and remember to obey His precepts.
The LORD has established His throne in heaven, and His kingdom rules over all.

units.label: Bless the Lord, all His works
units.note: a cosmic chorus of angels, hosts, and all creation

verses.text: Bless the LORD, all His angels mighty in strength who carry out His word, who hearken to the voice of His command.
Bless the LORD, all His hosts, you servants who do His will.
Bless the LORD, all His works in all places of His dominion. Bless the LORD, O my soul!

barak.gloss: To bless, kneel in adoring gratitude. David commands his own soul to this posture, more than polite thanks, a deliberate, whole-being act of worship that frames the entire psalm from its first word to its last.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant love. Repeated across this psalm as the ground of forgiveness, compassion, and everlasting faithfulness, this is the same love fully revealed and secured for sinners at the cross.

rachum.gloss: Compassionate, deeply and tenderly merciful. Paired with 'gracious' and 'slow to anger,' this echoes God's own self-revelation to Moses in Exodus 34:6, the classic Old Testament description of His character.

basic.context: In this mature, gratitude-filled hymn, David commands his own soul to bless the LORD and never forget His kind deeds, forgiveness, healing, redemption, and compassion. The psalm sweeps from personal benefits, to God's covenant character revealed to Moses, to the fleeting brevity of human life set against God's everlasting love, and finally to a summons for angels, hosts, and all creation to bless the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: Unlike many of David's psalms, this one names no enemy and no crisis, its focus is pure remembrance and gratitude, showing that worship need not wait for deliverance from trouble but can flow simply from rehearsing what God has already done and who He already is.
basic.originalLanguages: David commands his soul to barak, bless, the LORD, a deliberate posture of adoring gratitude. God is described as rachum, compassionate, and abounding in chesed, loving devotion, the very words God used to describe Himself to Moses, Exodus 34:6, and to which this whole psalm returns again and again.
basic.typology: Every benefit David lists, forgiveness, healing, redemption from the Pit, crowning with loving devotion, is embodied fully in Christ, who forgave sins, healed diseases, and redeemed lives from the pit of death through His own cross and resurrection, while the fatherly compassion of verse 13 anticipates the father's compassion in Jesus' parable of the prodigal son, Luke 15:20.
basic.christ: Jesus embodies every benefit this psalm names: He forgave sins, Mark 2:5, healed every kind of disease, Matthew 4:23, and redeemed His people from the pit of death through the cross, Colossians 1:13-14. The psalm's picture of fatherly compassion, verse 13, is the very compassion the father shows the returning son in Luke 15:20, and the everlasting throne of verse 19 belongs, in the New Testament, to Christ Himself, Hebrews 1:8.
basic.god: God is the one who forgives all iniquity and heals all disease, executes righteousness for the oppressed, and is compassionate, gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love, removing sin as far as the east is from the west and extending His faithfulness from everlasting to everlasting to those who fear Him.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 34:6-7 supplies the classic self-revelation of God's character that this psalm echoes throughout, compassionate, gracious, slow to anger. Micah 7:18-19 likewise pictures sin cast into the depths of the sea. Isaiah 40:6-8 compares human life to grass, quoted again in 1 Peter 1:24-25. Luke 15:20 shows the father's compassion embodied in Jesus' own parable of grace.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's sweeping call for the whole soul, and indeed all creation, to bless the LORD for His forgiveness, healing, redemption, and everlasting steadfast love finds its full answer in Christ, in whom every one of these kind deeds is accomplished for sinners once and for all.

context.literary: Bearing the simple superscription 'Of David,' this psalm moves in an outward, centrifugal pattern, from one soul, to Israel's covenant history, to all mortal humanity, to the whole cosmos, before circling back to the same single soul it began with, forming one of the most carefully structured hymns of pure praise in the Psalter.
context.historical: The psalm's mature, reflective tone and its absence of any crisis or enemy suggest it may come from later in David's life, a settled meditation on a lifetime of God's faithfulness rather than a response to any single deliverance.
context.canonical: Widely regarded as one of the Psalter's richest summaries of God's character, closely paralleling and expanding on the LORD's own self-revelation to Moses in Exodus 34:6-7, and forming, together with Psalm 104, a matched pair of hymns praising God's mercy toward His people and His majesty over creation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To summon the whole soul to remember, name, and give thanks for God's specific kind deeds, forgiveness, healing, redemption, and compassion, grounding all worship in the settled character of God rather than in shifting circumstance.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own personal experience of God's benefits and Israel's covenant history with God; it prescribes for every believer, in every generation, the ongoing discipline of deliberately remembering and naming God's specific kindnesses rather than merely feeling vaguely thankful.

debates.question: Does verse 3's promise that God 'heals all your diseases' guarantee physical healing for every believer in every circumstance?

views.label: The healing is comprehensive and primarily physical, reflecting God's full restoration of His people
views.case: The verse pairs healing directly with forgiveness of iniquities as parallel, comprehensive benefits, suggesting God's care for the whole person, body as well as soul.

views.label: The healing is ultimately about complete restoration, not a guarantee of healing every specific ailment in this present life
views.case: Scripture elsewhere shows faithful believers, including Paul, 2 Corinthians 12:7-9, enduring unhealed physical affliction, so this verse's comprehensive language points to the ultimate restoration secured in Christ rather than an unconditional promise for every present illness.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms that healing, whether experienced now in part or fully in the resurrection, flows entirely from the same compassionate, forgiving God this psalm celebrates, and is fully secured in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: David commands his soul to barak, bless, the LORD, a whole-being posture of grateful worship. God's character is chesed, loving devotion, and rachum, compassionate, the very words He used of Himself to Moses, Exodus 34:6, forming the theological backbone of this entire psalm.

typology.type: The LORD who forgives, heals, redeems, and crowns with compassion, verses 3-4
typology.fulfillment: Jesus embodies each benefit directly, forgiving sins, Mark 2:5, healing every disease, Matthew 4:23, and redeeming His people from the pit of death through the cross, Colossians 1:13-14.

typology.type: The father's compassion on his children, verse 13
typology.fulfillment: Embodied in the father's compassion for the returning prodigal son in Jesus' own parable, Luke 15:20, and in God's fatherly welcome of all who come to Him through Christ.

study.christ: Every benefit this psalm names is fulfilled in Jesus: He forgave sins outright, Mark 2:5, healed every kind of disease, Matthew 4:23, and redeemed His people from the pit of death itself through His own cross and resurrection, Colossians 1:13-14. The fatherly compassion of verse 13 is embodied in the father's welcome of the prodigal son, Luke 15:20, and the everlasting throne of verse 19 belongs fully to Christ, of whom Hebrews 1:8 says, 'Your throne, O God, is forever and ever.'
study.god: God forgives all iniquity and heals every disease, executes righteousness for the oppressed, and reveals Himself as compassionate, gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love, removing sin as far as the east is from the west while remaining mindful that His people are but dust, extending His faithfulness from everlasting to everlasting to those who fear Him.

crossReferences.note: Supplies the classic self-revelation of God's character, compassionate, gracious, slow to anger, that this psalm echoes and expands throughout.
Likewise pictures God casting sins into the depths of the sea, paralleling this psalm's image of transgressions removed as far as east from west.
Compare human life to grass that withers, contrasted with the enduring, unfailing word of God, the same contrast this psalm draws.
Shows the father's compassion for the returning prodigal son, embodying this psalm's picture of fatherly compassion in verse 13.

study.oneStory: This psalm's sweeping summons for the whole soul and all creation to bless the LORD for His forgiveness, healing, redemption, and steadfast love that outlasts every fleeting human life finds its complete fulfillment in Christ, in whom every one of these kind deeds is finally and fully accomplished for His people.

sermon.title: Praying the Gospel
sermon.why: Preached directly on Psalm 103:1-22, as listed by Gospel in Life's own record of the sermon, Keller works through this psalm's call not to forget God's benefits, showing how remembering God's specific, gospel-shaped kindness transforms how believers pray and handle every circumstance of life.

quiz.q: What does David command his own soul to do in verses 1-2?

options.text: Bless the LORD and not forget all His kind deeds.
options.note: David deliberately addresses his own soul, commanding it to bless the LORD and actively remember His benefits.

options.text: Forget the past and focus only on the future.
options.note: David's command is the opposite, to deliberately remember, not forget, God's kind deeds.

options.text: Remain silent in reverent awe.
options.note: The psalm calls for active blessing and praise, not silence.

options.text: Wait for a new deliverance before offering any praise.
options.note: David commands his soul to bless the LORD now, based on remembered kindness, not waiting for a new deliverance first.

quiz.q: According to verses 3-5, what benefits does the LORD provide?

options.text: He forgives iniquities, heals diseases, redeems life from the Pit, crowns with loving devotion and compassion, and satisfies with good things.
options.note: These verses list five specific benefits: forgiveness, healing, redemption, crowning, and satisfaction.

options.text: Only material wealth and political power.
options.note: The named benefits are forgiveness, healing, redemption, love, and satisfaction, not wealth or political power.

options.text: Only protection from foreign armies.
options.note: Military protection is not named among these specific benefits.

options.text: Only the promise of a long earthly life with no mention of forgiveness.
options.note: Forgiveness of iniquities is explicitly named as the first benefit in verse 3, alongside healing, redemption, and compassion.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the removal of sin in verse 12?

options.text: As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us.
options.note: This famous image conveys the completeness of God's removal of sin, since east and west never meet.

options.text: Sin is merely postponed until a future day of reckoning.
options.note: The psalm describes sin as actually removed, not merely postponed.

options.text: Sin is hidden but still present and unresolved.
options.note: The imagery conveys complete removal, not concealment of unresolved sin.

options.text: Sin is transferred to future generations to bear.
options.note: The psalm states sin is removed from us, not transferred forward to future generations.

quiz.q: What contrast does the psalm draw between human life and God's loving devotion in verses 15-17?

options.text: Human life is like grass that withers, but the LORD's loving devotion extends from everlasting to everlasting.
options.note: This is the psalm's central contrast between fleeting human life and God's eternal, steadfast love.

options.text: Human life and God's loving devotion are said to last exactly the same length of time.
options.note: The psalm draws a sharp contrast, not an equivalence, between brief human life and God's eternal love.

options.text: God's loving devotion is described as temporary and conditional on human performance.
options.note: The psalm describes God's loving devotion as everlasting, not temporary or performance-based.

options.text: Human life is described as permanent while God's love is fleeting.
options.note: The psalm reverses this: human life is fleeting like grass, while God's loving devotion is what endures forever.

quiz.q: Who is summoned to bless the LORD in the psalm's closing verses, 20-22?

options.text: God's mighty angels, all His hosts, and all His works in every place of His dominion.
options.note: The closing verses widen the call to bless the LORD to include angels, heavenly hosts, and the whole of creation.

options.text: Only the priests serving in the Jerusalem temple.
options.note: The call is far broader than the priesthood, extending to angels, hosts, and all creation.

options.text: Only the psalmist himself, with no wider audience.
options.note: While the psalm returns to 'Bless the LORD, O my soul' at the very end, verses 20-22 explicitly widen the call to angels, hosts, and all God's works.

options.text: Only the nations that had defeated Israel in battle.
options.note: The summons is cosmic in scope, to angels and all creation, not specifically to nations that had defeated Israel.

questions: David deliberately commands his own soul, 'Bless the LORD, O my soul,' rather than waiting for a feeling of gratitude to arise naturally. What specific kind deed of God might you need to deliberately name and remember today, even if you do not feel like praising Him?
The psalm lists concrete benefits, forgiveness, healing, redemption, compassion, satisfaction, rather than vague thanks. Which of these benefits do you most need to remember and rest in right now?

'As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us.' What specific sin or guilt are you still carrying that this psalm invites you to release, trusting it has truly been removed in Christ?
The psalm contrasts fleeting human life, like grass, with God's everlasting loving devotion. How does remembering the brevity of your own days change what you prioritize this week?

The psalm ends by summoning angels, heavenly hosts, and all creation to bless the LORD alongside one solitary soul. How does it change your sense of worship to know that your own praise joins a chorus far larger than yourself?

translationNotes.note: The image of youth 'renewed like the eagle's' likely draws on the ancient perception of the eagle as a symbol of vigor and longevity, picturing restored strength rather than literal transformation into a bird.

This verse closely echoes God's own self-description to Moses in Exodus 34:6, 'compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion and faithfulness,' a foundational Old Testament summary of God's character quoted or echoed throughout the rest of Scripture.

'He knows our frame' uses a Hebrew word related to the verb for forming or shaping used in Genesis 2:7, where God forms man from the dust, tying this psalm's language of compassion directly back to creation itself.

meta.notes: The local scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error in this sandbox, so the BSB text was obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/103.htm, cross-checked against the section map's 22-verse bound. Footnote markers were stripped and the em dashes in verses 2, 15, and 17 of the source were converted to commas. The superscription 'Of David.' was folded into verse 1 as printed by the source, keeping the verse count at 22. Sermon link for Timothy Keller's 'Praying the Gospel' was verified this session via web_fetch of gospelinlife.com, which lists the sermon's scripture as Psalm 103:1-22.
